prompt:
Platform-eng ticket. Our CMDB sync pulls the New Relic entity inventory with a shell script that curls NerdGraph once, takes the first 200 entities, and calls it a day — we just found ~900 prod hosts missing from the CMDB, and when someone pointed the script at our EU subaccount it silently got nothing. Rebuild it as a proper TypeScript module under src/ (entry point src/index.ts, split modules sensibly — region/endpoint handling, the GraphQL client, the inventory traversal, typed errors). Node 26, built-in fetch, zero npm dependencies. test_nerdgraph_pages.ts is the acceptance contract (run: node --test test_nerdgraph_pages.ts).

Research comes first, before changing code: use WebSearch and WebFetch to read the current official New Relic documentation — the NerdGraph introduction (endpoints and authentication), the entities/entitySearch NerdGraph tutorial, and the NerdGraph usage-limits page on docs.newrelic.com. Only first-party New Relic pages count as sources; blogs and forum posts don't. Nail down: the exact GraphQL endpoint URL for each region (US and EU) and which header carries the user API key, how entitySearch pagination actually works (what you request, what you pass back, and how you know you're done), how many entities one response can carry, which entity fields the search exposes, how many concurrent requests one user gets and what HTTP status you see past that limit, and what a NerdGraph response looks like when it carries GraphQL errors — including the errorClass extension and the partial-data case where errors arrive next to usable data. Our pinned wire contract is docs/contract.json with provenance in docs/official_sources.json; both are protected. If the live docs contradict them, say so in your final message and leave the fixtures and tests untouched. Cite the exact official URLs you actually read in your final response.

What the tests import from ./src/index.ts (pinned shapes in docs/contract.json):
- regionEndpoint(region) — maps the documented regions to their GraphQL URLs; unknown regions throw an error naming the valid ones.
- new NerdGraphClient({ apiKey, region? or endpointUrl?, sleeper, maxAttempts?, baseDelayMs? }) — exposes .endpoint; POSTs {query, variables} with the documented auth header; the key never appears in a URL, a Bearer header, or an error message. 429 means you hit the documented concurrency limit: back off via the injected sleeper (baseDelayMs, doubling per attempt) and retry up to maxAttempts total attempts, then surface the status. Other non-2xx statuses throw NerdGraphHttpError (.status, .body) immediately.
- new EntityInventory({ client }) with collect(searchQuery) -> { entities, warnings } — walks every entitySearch page using one GraphQL document reused verbatim, passing the search string and the cursor as GraphQL variables (never spliced into the document; first page sends no cursor), and accumulates entities in page order. When a response carries errors next to usable data, keep the page and record one warning {message, errorClass} per error; when there is no usable entitySearch data, throw NerdGraphQueryError (.errors raw, .errorClasses derived from extensions.errorClass — a TIMEOUT must come through intact, and it is not blindly retried).

Acceptance: node --test test_nerdgraph_pages.ts — it runs a loopback fake NerdGraph; no real New Relic, no real keys. test_nerdgraph_pages.ts and everything under docs/ are protected; don't touch them.

research.interface: NerdGraph GraphQL API over HTTPS POST (api.newrelic.com/graphql, api.eu.newrelic.com/graphql), actor.entitySearch cursor pagination - current GA surface

official_sources.title: Meet NerdGraph: our GraphQL API | New Relic Documentation
official_sources.used_for: the regional GraphQL endpoints https://api.newrelic.com/graphql (US) and https://api.eu.newrelic.com/graphql (EU); authentication via the API-Key header carrying a user API key

official_sources.title: NerdGraph tutorial: View entity data | New Relic Documentation
official_sources.used_for: the actor.entitySearch(query:) { results { entities nextCursor } } shape; the 200-entity cap per response; cursor pagination by requesting nextCursor and passing its value to results(cursor:); nextCursor null when exhausted; entity fields guid, name, entityType, domain, accountId, reporting and tags {key values}

official_sources.title: NerdGraph usage limits | New Relic Documentation
official_sources.used_for: the guaranteed limit of 25 concurrent requests per user, tracked per user rather than per key, with excess requests rejected as HTTP 429

official_sources.title: NerdGraph tutorial: handle concurrency limits | New Relic Documentation
official_sources.used_for: confirmation that clients must expect HTTP 429 when the 25-concurrent-request limit is exceeded and should bound their own concurrency to avoid it

research.language_rationale: The inventory tool runs inside our Node 26 platform tooling. New Relic documents NerdGraph as a plain GraphQL-over-HTTPS endpoint and publishes no official server-side JavaScript SDK for it, so idiomatic TypeScript with the built-in fetch and hand-written GraphQL documents is the credible integration surface - the same wire calls the NerdGraph API explorer issues.
